We observe 10 distinct lines. From shortest to longest wavelength: a pair of violet lines, a single blue-violet line, a single blue-cyan line, a single green-blue line, a strong single green line, a pair of yellow lines, a fainter orange-red line, and a single red line. This pattern of multiple lines across violet, blue, green, yellow, and red, with a prominent green line and a yellow doublet, is characteristic of a Mercury emission spectrum.
No flammable gases or combustion byproducts detected

The spectrum in image 6 features a prominent continuous rainbow band in its central region, smoothly transitioning from red through orange, yellow, green, and blue to violet. This central band exhibits no discrete emission lines.
To the far left of the continuous band, there is a single faint discrete emission line observed in the violet-blue region (shorter wavelength).
To the far right of the continuous band, there is a single distinct discrete emission line observed in the yellow-orange region (longer wavelength).

Observed 4 distinct emission lines. From left to right: a prominent violet line, a fainter dark blue/indigo line, a distinct cyan/light blue line, and a distinct red line. The pattern, number, and colors of these lines perfectly match the reference spectrum for Hydrogen.
Two lines are present in the violet/deep blue region on the far left of the spectrum, closely spaced.
One cyan-colored line is observed to the right of the violet/blue lines.
One prominent red line is present on the far right of the spectrum, indicating the longest wavelength line.
The characteristic pattern of two closely-spaced lines on the left, followed by a cyan line, and then a red line further to the right, matches the Balmer series.
A violet line is present on the far left.
A blue-violet line is located to the right of the violet line.
A cyan line is positioned further to the right, distinctly separated from the blue-violet line.
A red line is visible on the far right of the spectrum, with a large gap from the cyan line.
The overall pattern of four lines (violet, blue, cyan, red) and their relative positions and spacing match the Balmer series of Hydrogen.
The spectrum shows 4 distinct emission lines.
From left to right (shorter to longer wavelength), the lines are: a violet line, a blue line, a cyan line, and a red line.

The lines exhibit a characteristic pattern with two lines in the shorter wavelength region (violet at approximately 410 nm, blue at approximately 434 nm), followed by a cyan line (around 486 nm), and a significantly separated red line at the longer wavelength end (around 656 nm). This spacing and color sequence is consistent with the Balmer series of hydrogen.
The colors of the lines are violet (far left), blue (second from left), cyan (third from left), and red (far right).

The violet, blue, and cyan lines are grouped on the left side of the spectrum, with the blue line appearing close to the violet line, and the cyan line further to the right. There is a significant gap in the central and right-central part of the spectrum, followed by a prominent red line on the far right.
The spectrum displays 4 distinct emission lines.
The lines observed are: one violet line on the far left, one blue-violet line slightly to its right, one cyan line further to the right, and one red line on the far right of the visible spectrum.

The positions and colors of these lines correspond to the characteristic Balmer series of Hydrogen: approximately 410 nm (violet), 434 nm (blue), 486 nm (cyan), and 656 nm (red). The spacing and relative intensities are consistent with the Hydrogen reference spectrum.
